Equity Research · Initiation of Coverage
Aerospace & Defence
2026-07-04
Initiation of Coverage

Rheinmetall AG

XETRA (Deutsche Börse): RHM.DE
Defence prime€63.8bn backlogNet cashNATO 5% tailwindPost-F126 downtrend

Executive Summary

Rheinmetall is the primary listed vehicle for Europe's structural rearmament — a near-pure-play defence prime whose record €63.8bn order backlog (~6.4× revenue) underwrites a multi-year growth ramp with unusual visibility. The shares have de-rated ~37% over the past year on the June-2026 F126 frigate cancellation and a broader defence pullback, leaving them roughly fairly valued: in line with peers on forward earnings (~20× NTM P/E) and near the conservative intrinsic anchor, while a large DCF upside exists only if the aggressive management-ambition ramp (toward ~€50bn sales / >20% operating margin by 2030) is delivered. The stock is a high-quality growth compounder priced for a conservative terminal — the upside is optionality on execution and NATO budget flow, not a valuation discount.

Financial Snapshot↑

Metric LTM FY2026E (NTM)
Revenue (€m) 10,073 14,098
Adj. EBITDA (€m) / margin 2,076 / 20.6% 3,228 / 22.9%
Diluted EPS — net (€, FY2025) 15.16 36.70
EV / Net debt(cash) (€bn) ~51.7 / 0.8 —
Net debt / Adj. EBITDA 0.4× 0.2×
EV/LTM EBITDA · NTM P/E · FCF yield 24.9× · 20.1× · 1.7% —

As of 4 July 2026; price ~€1,093; ~46.5m diluted shares. FY2025 closed in net liquidity (+€369m); Q1 net debt reflects seasonal working capital + the €1.0bn NVL financing. Adj. EBITDA analyst-derived; peers reported.

Investment Thesis↑

  • Backlog-underwritten growth: €63.8bn backlog and ~2.0× book-to-bill give ~6.4× revenue coverage — the near-term ramp is contracted, not hoped for.
  • Munitions is the profit engine: Weapon & Ammunition earns ~34% EBITDA margin and ~51% of segment profit on ~32% of sales; its margin durability is the key swing factor for group margins reaching >20%.
  • Secular demand, not a cycle: the NATO 5%-of-GDP-by-2035 commitment and Germany's special funds convert into multi-year procurement — a regulation-led up-cycle.
  • Fairly valued with asymmetry: ~20× NTM P/E is in line with peers; the DCF bear (~€1,061) sits at the current price while the aggressive base (~€1,835) implies ~68% upside — limited downside, ramp-dependent upside.
  • Pristine balance sheet: net cash at FY2025, ~18× interest cover — capacity to self-fund capex and M&A (NVL naval) without leverage strain.

Key Strengths↑

  • Portfolio breadth (vehicles + munitions + electronics, now naval) with German home-nation status as Europe's largest budget re-arms.
  • Highest EBITDA margin (20.6%) and fastest forecast growth in the peer set; capacity-constrained munitions pricing power.
  • Record backlog and rising book-to-bill give multi-year revenue visibility few industrials match.

Key Risks↑

  • Terminal-value reliance: ~91% of DCF value is terminal; the base rests on an aggressive 2030 ramp and an 11× exit implying ~5.3% perpetuity growth (14× exit is the bull).
  • Munitions-margin normalisation: the ~34% W&A margin could compress as European capacity catches up — the single biggest downside driver.
  • Programme/political risk: the F126 cancellation shows single-programme risk even in a rising-budget environment; export-licence constraints apply.
  • Rich EV/EBITDA: 24.9× is a ~50% premium to the ~16.7× peer median; a growth or margin disappointment de-rates it.

Competitive Positioning↑

Rheinmetall is one of Europe's clear structural winners of the rearmament cycle — advantaged by portfolio breadth, munitions capacity (and vertical integration into energetics), and sovereign-supplier status. It out-grows and out-margins listed peers (BAE, Leonardo, Saab, Thales, Hanwha, Kongsberg, GD), which is why it commands an EV/EBITDA premium; its closest direct land-systems rival, KNDS, is private.

Entry Verdict↑

Fair Value — wait for trend repair or a better level. Rheinmetall is fairly valued (in line on forward P/E, near the conservative-intrinsic anchor) and technically in a post-F126 downtrend, with the shares ~17.5% below a falling 100-day EMA. With value neither cheap nor stretched, there is no urgency: prefer entry near support (~€929–€1,000) on further weakness, or on confirmation of a trend repair (MACD improvement, RSI reclaiming 50 back above the EMA at ~€1,325). The bull case is optionality on the rearmament ramp delivering toward management's 2030 ambition.

Fair Value — wait for trend repair

1Company Overview↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • High-quality, backlog-underwritten defence prime â€” â‚¬63.8bn backlog (~6.4Ã— revenue) makes the growth ramp contracted rather than speculative.
  • The central debate is munitions-margin durability: Weapon & Ammunition earns ~34% EBITDA margin and ~51% of segment profit on ~32% of sales â€” whether that holds as capacity scales drives the whole thesis.
  • Defining risk is single-programme/political exposure (the June-2026 F126 cancellation) and execution on an aggressive capacity build to 70,000 employees by 2028.

Anchor period: FY2025 (year ended 31 December 2025), reported on a continuing-operations basis (the civilian Power Systems business is classified as discontinued operations / held for sale). All figures € unless stated. "Operating result" is Rheinmetall's adjusted EBIT-equivalent management metric (EBIT before PPA effects and special items); "operating EBITDA" used below = operating result + depreciation & amortisation (calculated).

1.1Company Description↑

Rheinmetall AG is a Düsseldorf-headquartered defence prime contractor — Germany's largest — that develops and manufactures military vehicles, weapons and ammunition, and defence electronics for armed forces in Europe, North America and allied markets. It has repositioned from a mixed defence-and-automotive group into a near-pure-play defence supplier, and is divesting its civilian Power Systems (automotive components) division.

  • Revenue is generated primarily from long-cycle government defence procurement: platform builds (armoured vehicles), consumable munitions, and electronics/air-defence systems, increasingly underpinned by multi-year framework contracts.
  • FY2025 (continuing operations): Revenue €9,935m, operating result €1,841m (18.5% margin), operating EBITDA ≈ €2,230m (≈22.4% margin, calculated as operating result + D&A of €389m). Revenue rose 29% YoY; the order backlog reached a record €63.8bn.
  • The equity is a leveraged play on the European (and now US) rearmament cycle: FY2025 group sales grew 29%, and management's raised 2030 ambition is ~€50bn sales at a >20% operating margin.

Read-through: With a €63.8bn backlog covering ~6.4× FY2025 revenue, Rheinmetall's near-term growth is unusually well-underwritten for an industrial. The investment debate is less about demand existence than about conversion pace, margin mix and how much of the multi-year rearmament is already in the price.

1.2Product Offering↑

Rheinmetall packages hardware platforms, consumable munitions and electronic systems, sold mainly to national defence ministries and armed forces via direct government contracting and government-to-government frameworks.

  • Military vehicles — tracked and wheeled armoured platforms (e.g. the Boxer, Lynx KF41, and battle-tank/support programmes). See Vehicle Systems.
  • Weapons & ammunition — large- and medium-calibre ammunition, artillery, propellants, and weapon systems; a core beneficiary of the munitions-restocking wave. See Weapon and Ammunition.
  • Defence electronics — air-defence (e.g. Skyranger), sensors, fire-control, soldier systems, simulation and mission equipment. See Electronic Solutions.
  • Naval systems (new) — from the February 2026 closing of Naval Vessels Lürssen (NVL), Rheinmetall adds military shipbuilding, becoming a naval systems provider.

1.3Key Reporting Segments↑

Three continuing-operations segments. Shares below use external sales for the revenue mix and operating EBITDA (operating result + segment D&A) for the profit mix; segment EBITDA margins are calculated on segment (total) sales. FY2025, with FY2024 in brackets.

Segment Segment Description Segment Financials
Vehicle Systems — 49.3% of external revenue · ~31% of segment EBITDA · (14.7% EBITDA margin on segment sales, calc.) Tracked/wheeled armoured vehicles, logistics and tactical vehicles, and combat-vehicle systems (Vehicle Systems Europe + International). Largest revenue base but the lowest-margin segment — platform builds carry material content and programme ramp costs; margin is improving (operating margin 11.7% vs 11.2% FY24) as volumes scale. [chart · seg-fin-vehicle-systems]
Weapon and Ammunition — 32.3% of external revenue · ~51% of segment EBITDA · (34.0% EBITDA margin on segment sales, calc.) Large-/medium-calibre ammunition, artillery, propellants and weapon systems. The profit engine: ~a third of revenue but over half of segment EBITDA, at a ~34% EBITDA margin — consumable munitions demand, capacity-constrained supply and pricing power drive best-in-group economics. [chart · seg-fin-weapon-ammunition]
Electronic Solutions — 19.1% of external revenue · ~18% of segment EBITDA · (16.6% EBITDA margin on segment sales, calc.) Air defence, sensors, fire-control, soldier systems, simulation and mission equipment. Fastest-growing segment (external sales +44% YoY) with rising margins (operating margin 14.6% vs 12.6%); air-defence demand (e.g. Skyranger) is a key driver. [chart · seg-fin-electronic-solutions]

Other/Consolidation: external sales €(71)m, operating result €(144)m — holding/eliminations, not a segment.

Per-segment 2-year detail (Revenue · EBITDA · margin · YoY) is carried in the Segment Overview exhibit under Financial Overview.

1.4Geographies↑

Rheinmetall is structurally European, with Germany as the anchor market and a deliberate, accelerating push into North America. The geographic mix is shifting toward Germany and the Americas as domestic rearmament and the 2024 US Loc Performance acquisition scale.

  • Sales are aligned to four economic regions — Europe, Americas, Asia and the Near East — with 231 consolidated holdings in Germany and abroad and nine plants in the United States.
  • Germany rose to 38.0% of sales (from 34.2% FY24) as it takes a Framework-Nation role in European defence; the Americas jumped to 7.9% (from 4.1%), chiefly Loc Performance in the US.
  • NATO member states accounted for €7,433m (74.8%) of FY2025 sales, up 41.9% YoY — the customer base is overwhelmingly allied governments, which lowers credit and cancellation risk but concentrates exposure to European fiscal/defence-budget cycles.
  • Geographic risk: single-country (Germany) budget dependence, EU/German arms-export licensing regimes, and FX translation on a growing US footprint; offset by broad NATO diversification.

1.5Revenue Model↑

Revenue is predominantly long-cycle, contract-based government business, recognised over time as programmes and framework call-offs are delivered — high visibility, low churn, but lumpy on individual awards.

  • Contract/framework-based, not transactional: order intake of €19.9bn and total "Rheinmetall Nomination" of €26.4bn in FY2025 feed a €63.8bn backlog; framework backlog alone is €22.9bn, converting via multi-year call-offs (frame utilisation €5.0bn in FY2025).
  • Pricing: negotiated government contracts, increasingly with capacity-linked framework agreements (e.g. ammunition); consumable munitions carry the strongest pricing power in the current supply-constrained environment.
  • Revenue-model risks: dependence on government budget appropriations and export-licence approvals; individual programme cancellation risk (e.g. the June 2026 German cancellation of the F126 frigate programme — see Other Risks); backlog-conversion timing tied to capacity build-out.

1.6Customers / Sales & Marketing / Distribution↑

The customer base is national defence ministries and armed forces, sold direct and via government-to-government frameworks; NATO and European allies dominate.

  • Customer profile: overwhelmingly NATO/allied governments (74.8% of FY2025 sales to NATO members); named expanding relationships include Germany, Ukraine, Italy, Romania, Lithuania, UK, Poland, Denmark, Latvia and Bulgaria.
  • Customer concentration: a precise top-customer % is not disclosed; Germany as home nation and the largest single geography (38% of sales) is the effective concentration point.
  • Go-to-market: direct government contracting, strategic partnerships and joint ventures (e.g. the land-systems JV with Leonardo), and local plant footprints (US, and planned ammunition factories across Europe) to win domestic-content procurement.

1.7Demand Drivers↑

Demand is driven by a step-change in European and allied defence budgets, munitions restocking, and platform recapitalisation — largely secular/regulatory over this decade rather than purely cyclical.

  • NATO spending re-rating: the 2025 NATO agreement lifting the defence-spending ambition toward 5% of GDP (from 2%) underpins multi-year European budget growth; global defence expenditure is forecast to reach $3,086bn by 2030 (Jane's, cited in the annual report), with US spend ~$933bn.
  • Munitions restocking: depletion of stockpiles (Ukraine) and multi-year replenishment drives the highest-margin Weapon & Ammunition segment; management expects demand to "surge even more significantly in 2026."
  • Air-defence build-out: European air-defence gaps drive Electronic Solutions (Skyranger and related), the fastest-growing segment (+44% FY2025).
  • US programmes: XM-30 (combat-vehicle successor) and CTT (Common Tactical Trucks) are named multi-billion opportunities.

1.8Cost Drivers↑

The cost base is materials-heavy (defence hardware and munitions inputs) with a large and fast-growing skilled workforce; margins are levered to volume and capacity utilisation.

  • FY2025 (continuing ops) cost of materials €5,252m (52.9% of sales) and personnel costs €2,314m (23.3% of sales) — a materials-led cost structure (calculated ratios).
  • Key inputs include specialty steels/metals, propellants and nitrocellulose (Rheinmetall acquired nitrocellulose capacity to de-bottleneck ammunition supply), and electronics components.
  • What moves margins: capacity utilisation and mix (munitions vs vehicles), programme ramp costs (dilutive in Vehicle Systems), and pricing on framework renewals. Operating leverage is evident — group operating margin rose to 18.5% (FY25) from 12.8% (FY23).

1.9AI Considerations↑

AI is primarily an opportunity for Rheinmetall, embedded in products and operations, with modest direct disruption risk to its hardware franchise.

  • Product opportunity: AI/autonomy in mission systems, sensor fusion, targeting and drones, and simulation/training — Electronic Solutions is the natural home, and the annual report ties AI into R&D and air-defence/mission-system development.
  • Operational opportunity: AI in manufacturing/quality and supply-chain to accelerate munitions capacity ramp.
  • Risk: a shift in warfare toward cheap attritable autonomous systems could, over time, pressure the value of high-cost crewed platforms — a longer-dated substitution watch item rather than a near-term threat given order backlog.

1.10Research & Development↑

R&D scales with the platform and electronics portfolio but is modest as a share of sales for a defence prime, reflecting substantial customer-funded development.

  • FY2025 R&D expense €401m, 4.0% of sales (FY2024: €325m, 4.2%) — calculated ratio; €104m capitalised. R&D headcount 5,205 FTE (20.1% of the continuing-ops workforce).
  • By segment, Vehicle Systems carries the largest self-funded R&D (€256m), reflecting next-generation vehicle development.
  • vs peers: 4% of sales is toward the lower end versus electronics-heavy primes but typical for a vehicles/ammunition-weighted mix; a substantial share of defence development is customer-funded and not captured in reported R&D (analyst view — to benchmark against the approved peer set in the valuation section).

1.11Suppliers↑

Rheinmetall has moved to secure and internalise critical supply for its munitions ramp; concentration at the input level is a managed risk.

  • Supply-chain action: acquired nitrocellulose (propellant precursor) capacity to relieve a key ammunition bottleneck — evidence of vertical integration to protect volume growth.
  • Supplier concentration: a quantified supplier-concentration disclosure is not disclosed; specialty inputs (energetics, specialty metals, electronics) are the structural pinch points.
  • Risks: single-source energetic materials, geographic concentration of specialty inputs, and geopolitical/export exposure on cross-border components.

1.12Employees↑

Headcount is expanding rapidly as Rheinmetall builds capacity — one of the clearest signals of the demand it is underwriting.

  • Group FTE 32,251 at 31 Dec 2025 (FY24: 28,539); continuing operations 25,865 (FY24: 21,761), discontinued operations 6,387. By region: Germany 16,558, foreign 15,694.
  • By continuing segment: Vehicle Systems 9,363, Weapon & Ammunition 9,257, Electronic Solutions 5,765.
  • Management targets up to 70,000 employees by 2028 (from ~44,000 currently, group basis) — an aggressive capacity build.
  • Labour: as a large German industrial, the workforce is substantially covered by German co-determination and collective bargaining (IG Metall framework); specific CBA-renegotiation timing is not disclosed in the dataroom (flagged in gaps).

1.13Key IP or Facilities↑

The asset base is a growing network of vehicle, ammunition and electronics plants, expanding through greenfield munitions factories and acquisitions.

  • Facilities: manufacturing across Germany and Europe, nine US plants, and planned new ammunition factories across multiple European countries; PP&E of €1,962m at FY2025 (up from €1,853m) as capacity scales.
  • IP/brands: platform franchises (Boxer, Lynx, Skyranger) and proprietary ammunition/propellant technology; the Rheinmetall brand carries prime-contractor credibility with NATO customers.
  • Naval (new): the NVL acquisition adds Bremen-based military shipbuilding facilities (closed Feb 2026).

1.14Key Acquisitions and Divestitures↑

Most-recent-first, over the last ~2 fiscal years (the current disclosure window; the group was reshaped around defence).

  • Naval Vessels Lürssen (NVL) — acquired (closing 27 Feb 2026). Lürssen Group's military shipbuilding division (Bremen). Extends Rheinmetall into naval systems / shipbuilding; a term-loan club deal secured the purchase-price payment. Purchase price not disclosed in the dataroom.
  • Power Systems (civilian automotive components) — divestiture in progress. Classified as discontinued operations / held for sale (assets held for sale €1,270m at FY2025); completes the pivot to a pure-play defence company.
  • Loc Performance (US) — acquired November 2024. US military-vehicle/components manufacturer; the key milestone in scaling Rheinmetall's US footprint and content for US Army programmes (XM-30, CTT). Purchase price not disclosed in the dataroom.
  • Land-systems JV with Leonardo — established as a vehicle for European land-systems consolidation.
  • Purchase multiples are not calculable — prices not disclosed (flagged in gaps).

1.15Collateral Overview↑

Balance-sheet collateral is working-capital-heavy, characteristic of long-cycle defence manufacturing with large inventory and contract-asset build ahead of delivery.

  • Working-capital assets (12/31/2025): inventories €4,721m (up from €3,989m — building for the volume ramp), contract assets €957m (from €692m), trade receivables €1,907m (from €1,959m). Inventory is the dominant asset and the key working-capital swing as backlog converts.
  • Fixed assets: property, plant & equipment €1,962m (from €1,853m); right-of-use assets €338m.
  • Read: the large, rising inventory/contract-asset base is a direct function of the order ramp — a quality signal on demand but a cash-absorption watch item; operating free cash flow (continuing) was still positive at €1,218m in FY2025.

1.16Management Overview↑

An experienced, long-tenured CEO drove the defence pivot; a new CFO took the seat in January 2025.

  • CEO — Armin Papperger (Chairman of the Executive Board). CEO since 1 January 2013; reappointed for a further term. Architect of Rheinmetall's transformation into a defence pure-play and the capacity-expansion strategy.
  • CFO — Klaus Neumann. Took over as CFO effective 1 January 2025 (succeeding Dagmar Steinert). Other Executive Board members: René Gansauge (COO) and Dr Vera Saal (CHRO / Labour Director).
  • Chairman of the Supervisory Board — Ulrich Grillo (CEO of Grillo-Werke AG).
  • Background/controversy check: no material litigation, regulatory action or fraud involving the named individuals was identified in reliable third-party sources at the time of writing; no material issues identified (to be re-verified with a dedicated background search in diligence — flagged in gaps).

1.17Other Risks / Comments↑

Company-specific risks beyond the industry backdrop (industry-level risks are in the Industry Overview).

  • Programme-cancellation risk — live example: in late June 2026 Germany cancelled the F126 frigate programme, a contributor to the shares' ~37% decline over the prior year (per market data as of 4 Jul 2026); it illustrates single-programme political risk even amid a rising budget cycle.
  • Discontinued-operations drag: Power Systems generated a €(341)m loss from discontinued operations in FY2025; execution/price risk on the divestiture.
  • Valuation/expectations risk: the equity discounts a steep multi-year ramp (2030 targets of ~€50bn sales, >20% margin); any conversion or margin shortfall is amplified. (Assessed in the Valuation section.)
  • Capacity-execution risk: the workforce/facility build to 70,000 employees and the munitions-capacity ramp carry execution and fixed-cost-absorption risk if budgets or order timing slip.
  • Export-licence / political risk: German and EU arms-export approval regimes can constrain deliveries.

2Industry Overview↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • Structurally advantaged within a secular, regulation-led up-cycle â€” the NATO 5%-of-GDP-by-2035 commitment funds multi-year European procurement, and Rheinmetall's breadth + munitions capacity put it on the right side of the fastest-growing pools (155mm ammo ~19% CAGR).
  • The binding constraint is certified production capacity, which shifts pricing power to suppliers â€” Rheinmetall's greenfield ammunition plants and energetics vertical integration directly capture that excess margin.

Context: Rheinmetall is a European land-systems, weapons-&-ammunition and defence-electronics prime. This section frames the global and European defence market, its growth drivers, structure and competitive landscape. External figures are cited; company figures are from the dataroom.

2.1Industry Definition, Size & Characteristics↑

The relevant industry is land-focused defence systems and munitions within the broader global defence market — armoured vehicles, artillery and ammunition, and ground-based defence electronics/air-defence — sold to national governments. Naval systems become in-scope from Rheinmetall's 2026 NVL acquisition; combat aircraft and large naval platforms are adjacent and largely out of Rheinmetall's core.

  • Market size: global military expenditure was ~US$2,563bn in 2024, rising to ~US$2,689bn in 2025 (+4.9%), with forecasts of a ~5% CAGR to 2030–2035 (to ~$4,000–4,300bn by 2035) (MarketsandMarkets, accessed 4 Jul 2026; The Business Research Company, accessed 4 Jul 2026). The company's own filing cites Jane's forecasting global defence spend of ~$3,086bn by 2030 (US ~$933bn).
  • Character: historically cyclical and budget-driven, the industry is in a secular, regulation-led up-cycle — a structural re-arming of Europe rather than a normal cycle. It is consolidated among a handful of national/regional primes, with very high barriers to entry (security clearances, sovereign-supplier status, capital intensity, multi-decade programme relationships).
  • Scale/maturity markers: European NATO members lifted defence spending from 1.4% of GDP (2014) to 2.3% (2025), and in 2025 alone European Allies and Canada raised outlays by ~US$90–139bn (~+20% YoY) — the steepest climb since the 1950s (NATO, accessed 4 Jul 2026; SIPRI, accessed 4 Jul 2026).

2.2Structural Trends & End-Market Demand↑

The dominant trend is a multi-year, politically-mandated increase in European defence budgets, layered on munitions restocking and platform recapitalisation.

  • NATO 5% commitment (secular/regulatory): at the June 2025 The Hague summit, Allies committed to 5% of GDP on defence by 2035 — 3.5% for core defence plus 1.5% for broader security/infrastructure (NATO, accessed 4 Jul 2026; Agreement on 5% NATO defence spending — overview, accessed 4 Jul 2026). Germany's ~€100bn special fund (Sondervermögen) and successor budgets specifically fund land systems and ammunition.
  • Munitions restocking (cyclical→secular): the 155mm artillery ammunition market was ~US$7.1bn in 2025 (Europe ~48.8% of revenue), forecast to reach ~US$20.2bn by 2031 at a ~19.2% CAGR (GlobeNewswire/MarketsandMarkets, accessed 4 Jul 2026). This is Rheinmetall's highest-margin end-market (Weapon & Ammunition, ~34% EBITDA margin).
  • Land-platform recapitalisation (secular): Leopard 2 A8 tanks, IFVs (Lynx/CV90), and truck-mounted/self-propelled howitzers are being re-ordered at scale across Europe — Rheinmetall's Vehicle Systems end-market.
  • Air & counter-UAS defence (secular): European air-defence gaps drive short/medium-range systems (Skyranger and peers) — Rheinmetall's fastest-growing segment (Electronic Solutions +44% in FY2025).
  • Technology shift: drones/loitering munitions and counter-UAS, autonomy and AI-enabled mission systems are reshaping demand mix — an opportunity for electronics/ammunition and a longer-dated question mark for high-cost crewed platforms.

2.3Industry Structure & Value Chain↑

The industry is oligopolistic at the prime-contractor tier, organised nationally/regionally, with governments as both regulator and monopsony customer.

  • Concentration & barriers: a small set of primes dominate European land/defence — BAE Systems, Leonardo, Rheinmetall and Airbus Defence lead, with KNDS (Franco-German land-systems champion) and Saab as major players (EuroDev, accessed 4 Jul 2026). Barriers are structural: sovereign-supplier trust, security clearances, IP, capital-intensive certified production, and incumbency on multi-decade programmes. Precise CR4/HHI for the land sub-segment is not disclosed by reliable sources.
  • Value chain: upstream energetics/specialty metals & electronics → prime integration & platform/munitions manufacture (Rheinmetall's position) → government customers → through-life sustainment. Margin pools sit with (i) consumable munitions (pricing power, capacity-constrained) and (ii) sustainment/electronics; platform integration is lower-margin but high-content.
  • Structural shift in value capture: the current bottleneck is production capacity (energetics, shell lines), so value is shifting toward players who can add certified capacity fastest — Rheinmetall's greenfield ammunition factories and nitrocellulose vertical integration are a direct play on this.

2.4Competitive Landscape & Positioning↑

Rheinmetall is one of Europe's clear structural winners of the rearmament cycle, with breadth across vehicles, ammunition and electronics that few peers match.

  • Main competitors:
  • KNDS (Franco-German; private, potential IPO) — the closest land-systems/artillery competitor (Leopard 2, PzH 2000, Caesar, RCH 155) (Nordic Defence Review, accessed 4 Jul 2026).
  • BAE Systems (UK) — vehicles (CV90, BvS10), Archer howitzer, munitions (a top-tier 155mm producer).
  • Leonardo (Italy) — defence electronics and land systems; increasingly partnering with KNDS on Italian artillery (Defense Security Monitor, accessed 4 Jul 2026).
  • Hanwha Aerospace (South Korea) — a fast-rising artillery/vehicle exporter (K9 howitzer) competing hard for European orders.
  • Saab (Sweden), Thales (France), Kongsberg (Norway) — air-defence, electronics and missiles read-across; General Dynamics / Nammo in US/Nordic munitions.
  • Positioning: Rheinmetall was singled out among Europe's 2025 defence winners alongside Saab (Defense News, accessed 4 Jul 2026). Its edge is portfolio breadth + munitions capacity + German home-nation status as Europe's largest budget re-arms.
  • Market share: Rheinmetall holds leading positions in European large-calibre ammunition and land systems, but a precise, reliably-sourced market-share figure is not disclosed — stated shares in third-party reports are estimates and vary by sub-segment (flagged in gaps).

2.5Margin Dynamics: Bargaining Power & Key Constraints↑

Pricing power currently sits unusually far upstream with the primes, because demand vastly exceeds certified production capacity.

  • Supplier/capacity power (the binding constraint): the industry is capacity-constrained in energetics and shell manufacturing — the reason 155mm pricing and margins have risen. Whoever controls certified capacity (and its inputs, e.g. nitrocellulose) captures excess margin; Rheinmetall has moved to internalise this, supporting its ~34% EBITDA-margin munitions franchise.
  • Customer power (governments): as monopsony buyers, governments normally hold pricing power, but in the current shortage they are prioritising security of supply and speed over price — temporarily shifting power to suppliers. This can normalise as capacity catches up later in the decade (a margin-normalisation watch item).
  • Regulatory lever: export-licence regimes (German/EU) and sovereign-content preferences shape who can bid and deliver — an incumbency advantage for national primes like Rheinmetall, but also a delivery constraint.

3Financial Overview↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • Equity: a rare high-growth industrial â€” FY2025 revenue +29%, 20.6% Adj. EBITDA margin and mid-20s% ROIC â€” with margins set to expand toward >20% operating as munitions and electronics scale.
  • Credit: effectively unleveraged â€” net cash at FY2025, ~18Ã— interest cover â€” so the balance sheet funds the ramp and NVL M&A rather than a deleverage story; near-term FCF is thin (WC/capex build) but inflects hard from FY2028.
  • Watch item: FCF conversion stays low during the ramp (LTM FCF yield ~1.7%); the equity case needs the conversion recovery the model assumes from FY2028.

Continuing operations, IFRS, EUR millions. "Operating result" is the company's headline adjusted EBIT-equivalent (before PPA effects and special items); Adj. EBITDA is analyst-derived (operating result + depreciation & non-PPA amortisation). Clean continuing-ops 3-statement history is FY2024–FY2025 (Power Systems reclassified to discontinued operations); LTM = FY2025 + Q1'26 − Q1'25. Basis of preparation in Appendix §5.1.

3.1Financial Snapshot↑

Financial summary

Rheinmetall is a high-growth, cash-generative defence prime with a pristine balance sheet, its forward trajectory underwritten by a record €63.8bn order backlog (~6.4× revenue). The four panels below span FY2024 actuals through the FY2030E aggressive base case.

  • Revenue & growth — FY2025 sales €9,935m, +29% YoY on continuing operations; the base case compounds toward management's 2030 ambition.
  • Adj. EBITDA & margin (analyst-derived) — €2,064m at 20.8% in FY2025, expanding as scale and munitions mix lift margins toward >24% by FY2030E.
  • FCF & conversion — FY2025 total FCF €1,415m; near-term conversion is depressed by the working-capital build funding the volume ramp.
  • Net debt & leverage — FY2025 closed in net liquidity (+€369m); leverage is negligible (net cash to ~0.4×), a growth-investment balance sheet, not a deleveraging story.

3.2Summary Financials↑

($MM unless noted)FY2024FY2025LTMFY2026EFY2027EFY2028EFY2029EFY2030E

3.3Financial Trend Assessment & Analysis↑

Rheinmetall's financials describe a company in a step-change: revenue and profit inflecting sharply upward on European rearmament, margins expanding as the highest-value munitions business scales, and a balance sheet strong enough to self-fund an aggressive capacity build plus M&A. The equity read is a rare high-growth industrial compounder; the credit read is near-uninvestable-risk (net cash, ~18× interest cover) with the only watch item being the seasonal working-capital swing.

Adjusted vs GAAP. FY2025 GAAP EBIT €1,684m bridges to the €1,841m operating result via €167m of PPA amortisation (Expal/Loc Performance deals) and €9m special items; the €341m discontinued-operations loss (incl. a €376m Power Systems impairment) sits below continuing earnings and is excluded from the operating metrics used throughout. Adjusted metrics are the analytical default hereafter.

3.3.1Multi-year trends↑

Continuing-operations revenue rose from €7,715m (FY2024) to €9,935m (FY2025), +29%, with the operating-result margin lifting to 18.5% from 18.0% — operating leverage on a business scaling into a demand surge.

  • Revenue & mix — all three segments grew double-digit: Weapon & Ammunition +26% (the ~34%-EBITDA-margin profit engine, ~51% of segment profit on ~32% of sales), Vehicle Systems +30% (largest but lowest-margin), Electronic Solutions +44% (air-defence-led). Germany rose to 38% of sales; NATO members were 75%.
  • Margin & earnings — margin expansion is mix- and scale-driven (munitions and air defence out-growing lower-margin vehicles); ROIC is high (FY2025 Adj. NOPAT / (equity + net debt) ≈ mid-20s%), comfortably above any reasonable cost of capital.
  • FCF & balance sheet — FY2025 total FCF €1,415m; company-defined continuing-ops operating FCF €1,218m (the gap is discontinued-ops cash). The group ended FY2025 in net liquidity +€369m, aided by a capital increase (capital reserves rose to €1,394m from €696m) — deleveraging capacity is a non-issue; the balance sheet exists to fund growth.
  • Working capital — inventories (€4,721m) and contract assets (€957m) are the dominant, rising assets — a direct function of the order ramp and the key cash-absorption line.

3.3.2Most-recent interim↑

Q1 2026 (the latest print) shows the growth-and-investment tension cleanly: operating result +17% to €224m on +7.7% sales, but operating cash flow of −€155m as working capital built ahead of the FY2026 ramp.

  • Drivers — management attributed the Q1 operating-cash outflow to inventory build for targeted FY2026 revenue growth and to a tough prior-year comparison (Q1 2025 carried unusually high customer advance payments); it is seasonal timing, not deterioration.
  • Order book / visibility — order backlog reached €73.0bn at 31 March 2026 (from €55.6bn a year earlier); LTM book-to-bill ~2.0× and backlog coverage ~7.2× revenue — forward revenue is exceptionally well-covered.
  • Leverage — net financial position swung to ~−€829m (net debt) at Q1 from +€369m net liquidity at year-end, driven by the seasonal cash outflow and the €1.0bn club-deal draw funding the NVL naval acquisition (closed 27 Feb 2026); leverage remains well inside ~0.4× LTM Adj. EBITDA.

3.3.3Most-recent fiscal year↑

FY2025 delivered record sales and operating result on continuing operations, with management raising the 2030 ambition on the strength of demand.

  • Growth & mix — €9,935m sales (+29%), operating result €1,841m (+33%), margin 18.5%; growth was broad-based but munitions and electronics led both growth and margin.
  • Earnings quality — the headline net-income-to-parent of €696m is depressed by the discontinued Power Systems loss; continuing EPS (diluted €22.29) rose ~34% and is the cleaner read on earnings power.
  • Cash & capital allocation — capex €872m (8.8% of sales) funding capacity; dividends/distributions €421m; the €1.0bn NVL financing sits in Q1 2026. Management targets a 35–40% payout.

3.3.4Financial Forecasts↑

The base case is analyst-directed and deliberately aggressive, tracking toward management's raised 2030 ambition (~€50bn sales, >20% operating margin) while landing conservative of the full stretch at ~€42bn / 21.3% by FY2030E.

  • Base-case drivers — group revenue CAGR ~33% (FY26/27 calibrated to guidance and consensus, then decelerating); operating margin climbing 19.0% → 21.3% (munitions margin held near current levels); capex tapering from 9.0% to 5.5% of sales as capacity matures; 26% tax.
  • Cash & leverage path — FCF turns strongly positive as the WC build normalises; the group returns to net cash by ~FY2028 and net cash of ~€3bn by FY2030E — dividends (37% payout) are comfortably covered with room for further M&A.
  • Bull / Base / Bear — Bull ~€50bn / 22% (full ambition, munitions ~34%); Base ~€42bn / 21.3%; Bear ~€27bn / 18% (munitions margin normalises to the high-20s% as European capacity catches up, plus F126-type programme slippage). The single biggest swing factor is munitions (W&A) margin durability.

3.3.5Guidance↑

Management guides FY2026 group sales growth of +30–35% (continuing operations, organic) at an operating-result margin of 18.5–19%, and has raised its 2030 targets to ~€50bn sales, >20% operating margin, operating cash flow >50% of operating result and a 35–40% dividend payout.

  • Read-through — the base case (FY2026E ~€14.1bn, +42%) sits above the organic guide because it folds in the NVL naval acquisition; ex-NVL it lands within the guided range. Consensus (FY2026 €14.1bn, FY2027 €19.0bn) aligns with the base.
  • Direction — the 2030 ambition was raised (not lowered) at the FY2025 result, consistent with the rising backlog and the NATO 5%-of-GDP trajectory — supportive of both the equity growth thesis and the negligible-leverage credit profile.

3.4Segment Overview↑

SegmentSegment DescriptionSegment Financials

4Valuation & Entry↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • Fair Value: ~20Ã— NTM P/E is in line with the ~20Ã— core-comp median and the price sits at the conservative-intrinsic (bear) anchor â€” not a valuation-driven entry.
  • The mispricing debate is terminal-value reliance: ~91% of DCF value is terminal, and the market is pricing a conservative ~2.5% perpetuity (below the entire exit-multiple grid) â€” it is not paying for the â‚¬50bn 2030 ambition.
  • Entry: value fair, timing Wait â€” post-F126 downtrend (~17.5% below a falling EMA100); accumulate near â‚¬929â€“â‚¬1,000 support or on a confirmed trend repair above ~â‚¬1,325.

As of 4 July 2026; current price ~€1,093; ~46.5m diluted shares. Intrinsic figures from the step-4 dual DCF (narrated, not recomputed); relative multiples computed from inputs; peers reported EBITDA vs subject Adjusted (basis noted once).

4.1Overall Valuation Assessment↑

Rheinmetall is assessed Fair Value — roughly fairly priced on near-term relative multiples and against a conservative intrinsic terminal, with large but assumption-dependent upside if the aggressive rearmament ramp is realised. The debate is not whether the business is good but how much of a decade of growth to pay for today.

  • Intrinsic: the DCF base (exit 11×, aggressive ramp) implies ~€1,835/share (~68% upside), but the conservative FCFE-Gordon cross-check is ~€1,061 — essentially the current price. Value is ~91% terminal, so the base is a bet on the terminal, not the explicit cash flows.
  • Relative: RHM trades at 24.9× EV/LTM EBITDA vs a ~16.7× peer/core median — a ~50% premium — but at 20.1× NTM P/E, in line with the ~20× core-comp median. The EV/EBITDA premium is growth- and margin-justified (RHM's 20.6% EBITDA margin is the highest in the set; forecast growth ~2–3× peers).
  • FCF yield vs cost of capital: LTM FCF yield ~1.7% sits below the ~9% cost of equity and the ~2.5% Bund / ~4.2% UST risk-free — the current-year cash return is thin because capex and working capital are funding the ramp; FCF yield improves materially from FY2028.

Upside: (i) backlog €63.8bn / book-to-bill ~2.0× underwrites multi-year growth; (ii) munitions-margin durability drives group margin toward >20%; (iii) NATO 5%-of-GDP trajectory extends the runway. Downside: (i) the EV/EBITDA premium compresses if growth or munitions margin normalises; (ii) FCF conversion stays low longer than modelled; (iii) programme/political risk (F126-type cancellations).

  • Verdict: Fair Value — limited downside to a bear case near the current price, with meaningful, ramp-dependent upside; not a valuation-driven entry, but favourably asymmetric if the thesis holds.

Rheinmetall is fairly valued (roughly in line on forward P/E and near the conservative-intrinsic anchor) and technically in a post-F126-selloff downtrend â€” the shares sit ~17.5% below a falling 100-day EMA with weak momentum. With value neither cheap nor stretched, there is no urgency: wait for the trend to repair (MACD confirmation and RSI reclaiming 50 back above the EMA) or for a pullback toward support before initiating.

4.2Relative Valuation↑

CompanyPriceUS$Mkt capUS$MLTM RevUS$MLTM EBITDAUS$MEBITDA mgn%EV/EBITDAxNTM P/ExP/FCFxFCF yld%Div yld%

RHM screens as the highest-margin, highest-growth — and richest EV/EBITDA — name in a re-rated European defence peer set, but only middling on forward P/E.

As of 2026-07-04. Multiples computed from inputs (EV = mkt cap + net debt; FCF yield = FCF ÷ mkt cap). ¹BA.L NTM P/E excluded — Yahoo forward-EPS units glitch (pence/pounds) produced a spurious ~2,090× and would distort the stat.

Caveats: subject EBITDA is Adjusted (step-4 model); peers are reported (Yahoo) — this modestly flatters RHM's relative margin/multiple. Non-USD peers converted to USD (ratio columns FX-invariant). Core comps (weight these): BAE, Leonardo, Hanwha, GD. Hanwha/Kongsberg negative FCF → P/FCF n/m.

4.3Intrinsic Valuation↑

The dual DCF (FCFF → EV at 9.0% WACC; FCFE → equity at 9.0% Ke) values Rheinmetall on the analyst-directed aggressive base and an 11× exit multiple (lowered from 14× on a through-cycle review; 14× now carried as the bull).

  • Headline (base): Enterprise Value ~€85.6bn, Equity Value ~€85.3bn, ~€1,835/share (FCFF, exit 11×).
  • Scenario range: Bear ~€1,061 (conservative FCFE-Gordon, ≈ current price) · Base ~€1,835 (exit 11×) · Bull ~€2,291 (exit 14×, mature-prime re-rating; 16× ~€2,595 is the full-euphoria tail).
  • WACC: 9.0% = Ke (Rf 2.5% + β1.30 × ERP 5.0%); ~100% equity weight (net-cash balance sheet).
  • Diagnostics (the caveats that define the case):
  • Terminal value = ~91% of EV — the valuation rests overwhelmingly on the terminal, not the explicit 5-year cash flows.
  • FCFF vs FCFE gap ~42% — not a leverage artefact but the spread between the 11× exit (implying ~5.3% perpetuity growth) and a conservative 2.5% Gordon terminal.
  • Implied perpetuity growth ~5.3% on the exit multiple — above long-run GDP but below the ~6% embedded in the prior 14× base; defensible on continued high-terminal-year momentum.

4.4Football Field↑

Triangulating the three methods against the current €1,093 price shows wide divergence — the hallmark of a stock whose value hinges on terminal assumptions.

Method Low Mid High
DCF (FCFF) 1,061 1,835 2,291
EV/EBITDA Comps 594 732 1,958
NTM P/E Comps 1,011 1,118 3,075
52-Week Range (market) 929 – 2,008
Current price 1,093
  • The current price sits at the bear-DCF anchor and the NTM P/E-comps mid (~€1,100), i.e. fairly valued on a forward-earnings and conservative-intrinsic basis.
  • It sits above the EV/EBITDA-comps mid (~€732) — the ~50% EV/EBITDA premium is not supported by peer multiples on LTM EBITDA (though it is by growth).
  • The methods diverge sharply: the market is pricing the conservative terminal, not the aggressive base — the DCF upside is optionality on the ramp, not a consensus view. (Peer EBITDA is reported vs subject Adjusted.)

4.5Valuation Stress Test↑

The value is highly sensitive to the terminal assumption; both grids share WACC in the columns and the terminal parameter in the rows.

**Per share (€) — terminal growth (rows) × WACC (cols)** | g \ WACC | 8.0% | 9.0% | 10.0% | |---|---|---|---| | 1.5% | 1,135 | 967 | 839 | | 2.5% | 1,322 | **1,100** | 937 | | 3.5% | 1,592 | 1,281 | 1,066 |
**Per share (€) — exit EV/EBITDA (rows) × WACC (cols)** | × \ WACC | 8.0% | 9.0% | 10.0% | |---|---|---|---| | 9.0× | 1,594 | 1,531 | 1,471 | | 11.0× | 1,911 | **1,835** | 1,763 | | 13.0× | 2,228 | 2,139 | 2,054 |
  • Reverse-DCF read: the current €1,093 price sits below the entire exit-multiple grid (all cells ≥ ~€1,470) and within the Gordon grid (~€1,100 at 9% WACC / 2.5% g) — the market is implying a conservative ~2.5% perpetuity terminal, i.e. it is not paying for the €50bn 2030 ambition.
  • Terminal-value reliance (~91% of EV) means the base case is only as good as the exit multiple; the honest floor is the Gordon/FCFE leg near the current price.

4.6Technical Analysis↑

Technical read as of 2026-07-04. Rheinmetall is in a downtrend — the shares trade ~17.5% below a falling 100-day EMA following the late-June F126 frigate cancellation, with weak-but-stabilising momentum. The setup is unresolved (1 supportive / 2 conflicting signals), supporting a wait stance.

  • Trend: price €1,093.40, −17.5% below the 100-day EMA (~€1,325), which is itself falling (slope −4.5%) — a confirmed downtrend. Bollinger %B 0.45 (mid-band) — no stretch either way.
  • Momentum: RSI(20) 44 — weak (below the 50 line but off oversold). MACD histogram +3.46 — tentatively supportive, hinting at early stabilisation within the downtrend.
  • Volatility / volume: price mid-band (no squeeze or blow-off); volume 80% of the 20-day average — light participation, i.e. no conviction behind the recent moves.
  • What would change it: a clean MACD improvement with RSI reclaiming 50 on above-average volume, and price back above the EMA, would tilt the read toward a starter buy.

4.6.1Entry Assessment (combined)↑

Rheinmetall is fairly valued (roughly in line on forward P/E and near the conservative-intrinsic anchor) and technically in a post-F126-selloff downtrend — the shares sit ~17.5% below a falling 100-day EMA with weak momentum. With value neither cheap nor stretched, there is no urgency: wait for the trend to repair (MACD confirmation and RSI reclaiming 50 back above the EMA) or for a pullback toward support before initiating.

Preferred entry zone: €929–€1,000 (near the 52-week low on further weakness; alternatively confirm on a reclaim of the EMA100 at ~€1,325).

5Appendix↑ Contents

5.1Financials — Basis of Preparation↑

Reporting currency EUR; units EUR millions. Framework: IFRS, nature-of-expense income statement (no gross-profit line disclosed). Fiscal year end 31 December; quarterly reporting cadence. All figures are on a CONTINUING-OPERATIONS basis: the civilian Power Systems (automotive components) business is classified as discontinued operations / held for sale (assets held for sale EUR 1,270m at FY2025) and excluded from the operating metrics. Clean continuing-ops three-statement history therefore spans FY2024-FY2025 (both restated in the FY2025 Annual Report); pre-FY2024 detail was reported on the old total-group basis (incl. Power Systems) and is not carried like-for-like â€” longer-run group trend is drawn from the company's 7-year key-figures table in the overview. Segments are on the FY2025 3-segment basis (Vehicle Systems, Weapon & Ammunition, Electronic Solutions); note the FY2026 interims re-segment to five segments (Electronic Solutions split into Air Defence + Digital Systems; Naval Systems added from the NVL acquisition), so projections are built at group level. Headline non-GAAP measure = the company's 'operating result' (adjusted EBIT-equivalent, before PPA amortisation effects and special items); Adjusted EBITDA is ANALYST-DERIVED (operating result + depreciation & non-PPA amortisation) and used for leverage/coverage. LTM anchor = FY2025 + Q1 2026 - Q1 2025 (LTM net income to parent EUR 723m ties to third-party TTM). Projection base = FY2025 (the last actual full year); forward interest is charged on opening debt (circular-free), cash is the CFS-derived plug, and non-debt liabilities (contract liabilities/advances/provisions/payables) balance the projected balance sheet.

5.2Key Assumptions & Methodology↑

5.2.1Financial Model Assumptions↑

Assumption Value Tag Basis
Group revenue growth +42% / +35% / +32% / +30% / +29% (FY26E-FY30E) sourced+assumed FY26/27 to guidance +30-35% & consensus (incl. NVL); accelerated toward mgmt EUR 50bn 2030 ambition (analyst-directed aggressive base)
Operating-result margin 19.0% -> 21.3% (FY26E-FY30E) sourced+assumed FY26 guide 18.5-19%; mgmt >20% by 2030; munitions margin held
D&A % of sales 3.9% -> 3.5% assumed scale leverage on FY25 4.1%
Capex % of sales 9.0% -> 5.5% assumed elevated capacity build tapering as capacity matures (FY25 8.8%)
Tax rate 26% sourced FY25 effective 25.2%
Interest rate on opening debt 4.5% assumed current marginal borrowing
Debt schedule +EUR 1.0bn FY26 (NVL club deal), then -EUR 150m/yr sourced+assumed Q1'26 disclosed EUR 1.0bn draw; modest paydown thereafter
NVL consideration ~EUR 1.0bn, debt-funded assumed price not disclosed; inferred from the club-deal draw
Dividend payout 37% of net income to parent sourced stated 35-40% policy
Working capital NWC = 18% of incremental sales (cash outflow) assumed inventory/contract-asset-heavy defence build
Diluted shares ~46.5m, flat sourced post convertible conversion
Terminal (DCF) Exit EV/EBITDA 15.0x on FY2030E EBITDA; WACC ~9%; g 2.5% (FCFE leg/stress) assumed analyst-selected exit-multiple method; below RHM's ~24x current; implied-g cross-check at Gate B

5.2.2Comps & Relative-Valuation Methodology↑

Peer set = 7 approved defence primes (core: BAE Systems BA.L, Leonardo LDO.MI, Hanwha Aerospace 012450.KS, General Dynamics GD; read-across: Saab SAAB-B.ST, Thales HO.PA, Kongsberg KOG.OL). Sourcing: Yahoo Finance (AV COMPANY_OVERVIEW unavailable for these non-US listings); subject LTM from the step-4 model report_pack.json (label-keyed). All multiples computed from inputs (EV = market cap + net debt; FCF yield = FCF / market cap; NTM P/E = price / consensus forward EPS); no vendor multiples lifted. Non-USD peers converted to USD at the rates stamped in comparables.md; ratio columns are FX-invariant. Basis caveat: subject EBITDA is Adjusted (analyst-derived); peers are reported EBITDA (Yahoo) â€” modestly flatters the subject's relative margin/multiple. BA.L NTM P/E excluded (Yahoo forward-EPS pence/pounds units glitch produced a spurious ~2,090x). Hanwha and Kongsberg negative FCF -> P/FCF n/m; Hanwha no forward EPS -> NTM P/E n/a. Weight the Core-comp rows over the full-peer stats.

5.3DCF Assumptions, WACC Build & Output Detail↑

Dual DCF (FCFF -> EV at WACC; FCFE -> equity at Ke). Base case uses an exit-multiple terminal of 11.0x (lowered from 14.0x on a through-cycle review) on the analyst-directed aggressive projection; 14.0x is now the bull. Terminal value ~91% of EV; FCFF-vs-FCFE reconciliation gap ~42% reflects the exit multiple (implied ~5.3% perpetuity g) vs the conservative 2.5% Gordon FCFE terminal. Reverse-DCF: the current price implies a conservative ~2.5% perpetuity terminal (sits within the Gordon grid, below the entire exit-multiple grid).

Item Value Tag Basis
Risk-free rate 2.5% sourced German 10-yr Bund
Equity risk premium 5.0% assumed mature-market premium
Levered beta 1.30 assumed growth defence profile (reported low-beta history unrepresentative)
Cost of equity / WACC 9.0% / 9.0% assumed Rf + beta x ERP; ~100% equity weight (net-cash)
Cost of debt (pre-tax) 4.5% assumed current marginal borrowing
Terminal method Exit EV/EBITDA 11.0x (g 2.5% FCFE leg) sourced analyst-selected; through-cycle review (14x->11x)

Per share Bear / Base / Bull EUR 1,061 / 1,835 / 2,291 computed FCFE-Gordon / FCFF exit 11x / FCFF exit 14x

  • Market data — Yahoo Finance â€” peer & subject market data (price, market cap, EBITDA, forward EPS) (accessed 2026-07-04)
  • Risk-free rate — German 10-yr Bund yield (~2.5%) / US 10-yr Treasury (~4.2%) reference (accessed 2026-07-04) Price and indicator data sourced from Yahoo Finance via yfinance (1-year daily). Indicators: EMA(100) with slope, Bollinger Bands (20-period, 2 sigma) and %B, Wilder RSI(20), MACD(12/26/9), Volume vs 20-day average. Signal: regime classifier (trend from price vs EMA100 and EMA slope) feeding a 4-action decision tree (accumulate / starter buy / wait / avoid adding) with a confidence overlay. Chart as of 2026-07-04.

6Disclosures↑ Contents

Analyst certification. The views expressed in this report accurately reflect the analyst's personal views about the subject security and issuer; no part of compensation was, is, or will be directly or indirectly related to the specific recommendation herein.

Rating scale (entry, not a price target). This report uses a three-point valuation scale — Attractive · Fair Value · Expensive — reflecting intrinsic value versus the current price combined with relative multiples and FCF yield versus cost of capital. It is not a price target. Entry timing (accumulate / starter buy / wait / avoid adding) is a separate technical overlay.

Important disclosures. This is an initiating-coverage research illustration prepared from public filings (Rheinmetall FY2025 Annual Report, Q1 2026 quarterly statement) and third-party market data (Yahoo Finance, stockanalysis.com, and cited industry sources) as of 4 July 2026. Figures are in EUR unless stated; financials are on a continuing-operations basis. The financial model contains analyst estimates and an analyst-directed aggressive base case.

Disclaimer. For information only; not investment advice, nor an offer or solicitation to buy or sell any security. Forward-looking statements are subject to risks and uncertainties; actual results may differ materially. The analyst and firm may hold positions in the securities mentioned. Technical analysis is presented as research illustration only.
